content:
A comparison across 59 tabular data sets shows that the choice of hyperparameter optimization method matters — and that small tuning budgets are often not enough

8 min read
Just now

--

Tree-boosting methods such as LightGBM and XGBoost are among the strongest general-purpose tools for tabular data. They can learn nonlinearities, interactions, and discontinuities, often with little preprocessing. But their predictive accuracy can depend heavily on hyperparameters such as the learning rate, tree size, regularization, subsampling fractions, and the number of boosting iterations.
This creates a practical problem. There are many possible configurations, and evaluating each one requires fitting another model. Practitioners therefore need a search strategy that finds accurate hyperparameters with a limited tuning budget. Common choices include random search, Bayesian optimization, Hyperband, and more specialized model-based optimizers. Yet it is often unclear which one works best for tree-boosting in practice. Many applied studies still rely on default values, small ad-hoc grids, or a small number of trials.
In our recent article, “Selecting Hyperparameters for Tree-Boosting,” Floris Jan Koster and I compare several popular hyperparameter optimization (HPO) methods across 59 regression and binary classification data sets. The main result is clear: SMAC provides the best average predictive accuracy in our experiments. Figure 1 illustrates our results visually. The study also gives several broader practical lessons:

• Accurate tuning often requires more than 100 trials.

• Default hyperparameters and a deterministic search over a small grid can lead to substantially worse models.

• There is no small subset of hyperparameters that can safely receive all the attention.

• For regression, choosing the number of boosting iterations through early stopping is generally better than treating it as another search parameter.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Figure 1: Normalized out-of-sample scores as a function of the number of trials for different hyperparameter optimization methods. Higher values are better for R² and accuracy; lower values are better for RMSE and log loss. The confidence intervals represent uncertainty across data sets.Why hyperparameter tuning is difficult
A tree-boosting model is built sequentially. Each new tree attempts to improve the current model, but many design choices determine how this happens. The learning rate controls how strongly each tree changes the prediction. The number of leaves and maximal depth control tree complexity. The minimum number of observations per leaf and regularization penalties limit overfitting. Feature and row subsampling introduce additional regularization. Even the number of histogram bins can affect the fitted splits.
These hyperparameters also interact. A small learning rate may work well with many trees, while a large learning rate may require fewer trees. Deep trees may require stronger regularization. The best feature-subsampling rate may depend on the number and quality of available predictors. This makes tuning difficult to reduce to a few independent one-dimensional choices.
The hyperparameter optimization methods compared
We compare six tuning methods, as well as using the LightGBM defaults.
Random grid search
Random search samples hyperparameter combinations from a predefined grid. Unlike deterministic grid search, it does not spend trials systematically evaluating every combination of a few selected values. Its main advantage is simplicity, and it often performs surprisingly well.
Deterministic grid search
A deterministic grid evaluates all combinations from a fixed set of candidate values. This is intuitive, but it becomes inefficient in higher-dimensional search spaces. Adding just a few values for each new hyperparameter can make the grid grow very quickly.
Gaussian-process Bayesian optimization
Gaussian-process-based Bayesian optimization builds a probabilistic model of the validation loss as a function of the hyperparameters. It then uses this model to decide which configuration to evaluate next. The goal is to balance exploration of uncertain regions against exploitation of promising ones.
Tree-structured Parzen estimator (TPE)
The tree-structured Parzen estimator, or TPE, also uses previous trials to guide future evaluations. Rather than directly modeling the validation loss, it compares the distributions of hyperparameters associated with good and poor configurations.
Hyperband
Hyperband allocates different amounts of computational resources to different configurations. It starts by evaluating many candidates cheaply and progressively discards poor performers, giving more resources to the remaining configurations. This can work well when performance at a small resource budget is informative about final performance.
SMAC
The sequential model-based algorithm configuration method, or SMAC, uses a random forest surrogate model to predict the validation performance of candidate hyperparameters. We use SMAC3’s standard HPO interface with a random-forest surrogate and expected improvement to select new configurations.
The experiment
The comparison uses 59 publicly available OpenML data sets:
• 36 regression tasks
• 23 binary classification tasks
The data sets differ in sample size, number of predictors, signal strength, and response type.
For each data set, the methods are evaluated using five train-test splits. Hyperparameters are selected using a further training-validation split inside each training set. The final model is then retrained on the complete training portion and evaluated on the held-out test data.
Most optimization methods contain randomness. To measure its effect, the searches are repeated with 20 different random seeds. The main comparison uses a budget of 135 trials for random search, TPE, Gaussian-process Bayesian optimization, SMAC, and deterministic grid search. Hyperband receives a roughly comparable total boosting-iteration budget.
The evaluated hyperparameters include:

• learning rate

• number of leaves

• maximal tree depth

• minimum number of observations per leaf

• L2 regularization

• number of histogram bins

• row-subsampling fraction

• feature-subsampling fraction
The number of boosting iterations is usually chosen through early stopping rather than included directly in the search space, but we also compare what happens when we include it in the search space.
SMAC performs best on average
Across all considered evaluation measures, SMAC achieves the best average out-of-sample performance. Figure 1 above visualizes this. The figure shows test accuracy scores as a function of the number of tuning trials for different HPO methods. The scores are appropriately normalized to make them comparable across data sets. Task-specific results and other forms of normalization for comparison across data sets can be found in the full article.
We see that, for regression, SMAC achieves the best results for both RMSE and R². For classification, it also performs best for both accuracy and log loss. TPE is generally the second-strongest method. Random search is close to TPE for regression, while Gaussian-process Bayesian optimization is competitive for classification. Deterministic grid search and Hyperband perform considerably worse in the considered setup.
The precise ranking varies across individual data sets. SMAC is not best on every individual task, although it ranks first on many of them. The result should therefore be interpreted as a strong average recommendation rather than a universal guarantee. One plausible explanation for SMAC’s performance is its random forest surrogate. The tree-boosting search space contains continuous and discrete parameters, strong interactions, and potentially irregular validation-loss surfaces. Random forests are well suited to modeling this type of structure.
More than 100 trials may be needed
A common practice is to tune a model using perhaps 20 or 50 trials. Our results suggest that this is often insufficient. For SMAC, TPE, Gaussian-process Bayesian optimization, and random search, average performance continues to improve as the number of trials approaches 100 and beyond. A small tuning budget can therefore have two consequences. There is no universal trial count that is sufficient for every data set. But the results indicate that 100 trials should not be viewed as an unusually large budget for serious tuning of tree-boosting models.
Default hyperparameters often perform poorly
The LightGBM defaults perform substantially worse than all tuned alternatives on average. Defaults are necessarily generic. They are designed to provide reasonable behavior across many problems, not to be optimal for a specific data set. The performance gap is particularly important because tree-boosting is sometimes described as a method that works well “out of the box.” This may be true relative to models requiring extensive preprocessing, but it does not mean that tuning is unimportant. Using default hyperparameters can leave considerable predictive accuracy on the table.
No small subset of hyperparameters dominates
Another common strategy is to tune only the learning rate and tree size while leaving all other parameters fixed. In our experiments, the deterministic full grid search, which tunes only a small subset of hyperparameters over a coarse grid, performs poorly on average. However, this result alone does not show that tuning only a few parameters is the cause, because the coarseness of the grid may also matter. To investigate this question further, we have analyzed how validation performance varies with the hyperparameters across all trials and data sets.
The results do not reveal a small dominant subset. Some hyperparameters are more influential than others, but all considered hyperparameters are associated with meaningful variation in validation performance. The differences in average importance are not large enough to conclude that most parameters can safely remain fixed at their default values. For general-purpose tuning, it is therefore safer to use a reasonably broad search space.
Use early stopping for regression
The number of boosting iterations can be handled in two ways. One option is to include it directly in the hyperparameter search space. Each trial then evaluates a fixed number of trees. The other option is early stopping. For each candidate configuration, trees are added until the validation score has not improved for a specified number of rounds. We have compared both options.
For regression, early stopping clearly performs better in the experiments. This is intuitive because the optimal number of trees depends strongly on the learning rate and other complexity parameters. Early stopping adapts the number of iterations separately for every candidate configuration rather than forcing the optimizer to discover this interaction through additional trials. For classification, the results are less uniform. Including the number of iterations in the search space can be competitive for some optimization methods.
Practical recommendations
For practitioners using LightGBM-style tree-boosting on tabular data, the study supports the following strategy:

• Use SMAC when a stable general-purpose hyperparameter optimizer is available.

• Allocate a substantial tuning budget. Fewer than 100 trials may be insufficient, especially for broad search spaces.

• Do not rely on default hyperparameters when predictive accuracy matters.

• Tune a broad collection of parameters rather than only the learning rate and tree depth.

• For regression, use early stopping to choose the number of boosting iterations.
TPE, Bayesian optimization, and random search remain useful alternatives.
Limitations
The study focuses on the LightGBM tree-boosting algorithm as implemented in GPBoost. The ranking could differ for other boosting libraries or substantially different search spac
